content.text: What the Compliance Dashboard Shows

The Azure Policy Compliance Dashboard in the Azure portal is the primary surface for viewing policy compliance state across your management hierarchy. It aggregates data from the PolicyInsights store and presents it as a single compliance percentage (the overall score), per-assignment breakdowns, per-policy breakdowns within initiatives, and resource-level drill-downs. The dashboard reflects the last completed background evaluation scan, which runs every 24 hours — it is not real-time.

The overall compliance score formula is: Compliant / (Total - Exempt - Not Started). Resources in the Exempt state are intentionally excluded from the denominator because they have an approved exemption. Resources in the Not Started state are excluded because they have not yet been evaluated (new assignments, or resources in excluded scopes). The score therefore reflects the fraction of evaluated resources that are in compliance.

A policy assignment with Effect: Deny will show 100% compliance with 0 resources — Deny prevents resources from entering NonCompliant state in the first place. This is correct behavior, not a display bug.
Navigating the Dashboard

The dashboard supports hierarchical drill-down. At the top level you see the aggregate score for the selected scope. Clicking an assignment row shows per-policy compliance within that assignment (for initiatives) or per-resource state (for individual policies). Clicking a resource row shows every policy assignment that evaluated that resource and the resulting state for each.
View
What It Shows
How to Access
Overall compliance
Aggregate score + per-assignment rows
Azure Policy → Compliance (top-level)
Assignment detail
Per-policy compliance within an initiative assignment
Click the assignment name in the compliance list
Policy detail
Per-resource compliance state for one policy
Click the policy name inside an assignment detail view
Resource detail
All assignments evaluated against one resource
Click a resource ID in the policy detail view or search by resource
Initiative compliance mapping
Per-control compliance grouped by policyDefinitionGroups (CIS, NIST, etc.)
Regulatory Compliance blade (Defender for Cloud) or policyDefinitionGroups in assignment detail
Querying Dashboard Data Programmatically

The dashboard data comes from the PolicyInsights store, accessible via Azure CLI, REST API, and Resource Graph. Programmatic access is essential for building custom reports, integrating compliance metrics into ITSM tools, and building automated alerting that is not possible through the portal alone.

# Get the overall compliance summary for a management group
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query '{
    Compliant: results.resourceDetails[?complianceState==`Compliant`].count | [0],
    NonCompliant: results.resourceDetails[?complianceState==`NonCompliant`].count | [0]
  }' -o json

# Per-assignment compliance summary (mirrors the dashboard assignment rows)
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'policyAssignments[].{
    Assignment: policyAssignmentId,
    Compliant: results.resourceDetails[?complianceState==`Compliant`].count | [0],
    NonCompliant: results.resourceDetails[?complianceState==`NonCompliant`].count | [0]
  }' -o table

# List all NonCompliant resources for a specific assignment
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant' and policyAssignmentName eq 'corp-baseline'" \
  --select resourceId,resourceGroup,subscriptionId,policyDefinitionName \
  -o table

# Trigger a fresh evaluation scan before querying (avoids 24h-old stale data)
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID
# Note: MG-scope trigger-scan is not supported — trigger per subscription
# For large MGs, loop over subscription IDs:
for sub in $(az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv); do
  echo "Triggering scan for $sub"
  az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $sub --no-wait
done
Exporting Compliance Data

The compliance dashboard supports CSV export directly from the portal for the current view. For bulk exports, automated reporting, or feeding data into external systems (Splunk, Sentinel, Power BI), the PolicyInsights REST API and Resource Graph are the correct approaches. The REST API export method returns paginated JSON; Resource Graph returns a flat table that is easier to process.

# Export compliance state via REST API (paginated — use nextLink to get all pages)
az rest \
  --method post \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.PolicyInsights/policyStates/latest/queryResults?api-version=2019-10-01" \
  --body '{
    "$filter": "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'",
    "$select": "resourceId,resourceGroup,subscriptionId,policyAssignmentId,policyDefinitionId,complianceState",
    "$top": 1000
  }' \
  --query 'value' -o json > noncompliant_export.json

# Resource Graph export — simpler, max 1000 results per query (use pagination for more)
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| project
    resourceId       = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    sub              = subscriptionId,
    rg               = tostring(properties.resourceGroup),
    assignment       = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName),
    policy           = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName),
    effect           = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionAction)
| order by sub asc, rg asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table > dashboard_export.tsv

# Feed compliance data into a Log Analytics workspace for long-term trending
# (use Azure Monitor Diagnostic Settings on Policy to export automatically)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --name export-policy-to-law \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --resource-type Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates \
  --workspace $LAW_RESOURCE_ID \
  --logs '[{"category": "PolicyStateChangeEvents", "enabled": true}]'
Understanding the Compliance Score Formula

The compliance score displayed in the dashboard is not simply Compliant / Total. It excludes two resource categories from the denominator: Exempt and Not Started. This means a scope with many exemptions can show a higher compliance score than expected from looking at raw resource counts.

# Manual compliance score calculation matching what the dashboard shows
# Formula: compliant / (compliant + nonCompliant)
# (Exempt and Not Started are excluded from denominator)

SUMMARY=$(az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails' -o json)

COMPLIANT=$(echo $SUMMARY | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(next((x['count'] for x in d if x['complianceState']=='Compliant'),0))")
NONCOMPLIANT=$(echo $SUMMARY | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(next((x['count'] for x in d if x['complianceState']=='NonCompliant'),0))")
EXEMPT=$(echo $SUMMARY | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(next((x['count'] for x in d if x['complianceState']=='Exempt'),0))")

echo "Compliant:    $COMPLIANT"
echo "NonCompliant: $NONCOMPLIANT"
echo "Exempt:       $EXEMPT"
echo "Score:        $(python3 -c "c=$COMPLIANT; n=$NONCOMPLIANT; print(f'{100*c/(c+n):.1f}%' if c+n>0 else 'N/A')")"

# Note: the dashboard score resets if you trigger-scan — it reflects the most
# recent completed scan, not an in-progress one. During scanning, some resources
# may temporarily show 'Unknown' state.
Custom Workbooks for Compliance Reporting

Azure Monitor Workbooks can visualize PolicyInsights data with custom charts, trend lines, and cross-subscription aggregations that the built-in compliance dashboard cannot provide. A workbook can combine compliance state data with resource metadata, cost information, and CMDB tags to produce executive-level compliance reports. Workbooks are saved in a Log Analytics workspace or an Application Insights instance and can be shared as report templates.

# Create a compliance workbook from a template JSON
az monitor workbook create \
  --name 'Policy Compliance Report' \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --category workbook \
  --kind shared \
  --serialized-data @workbook-policy-compliance.json

# Useful Resource Graph queries to embed in workbooks:

# 1. Compliance trend by assignment over time (requires Azure Monitor / LA data)
# Query from PolicyStateChangeEvents in Log Analytics:
# AzureActivity
# | where OperationNameValue == 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/policyStates/triggerEvaluation/action'

# 2. Top 10 policy definitions causing the most NonCompliant resources
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| summarize Count = count() by policyDef = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName)
| order by Count desc
| limit 10
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# 3. Compliance by subscription (for exec dashboard)
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(tostring(properties.complianceState) == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(tostring(properties.complianceState) == 'NonCompliant')
    by sub = subscriptionId
| extend Score = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)
| order by Score asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# 4. Resources that have been NonCompliant for over 7 days
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where todatetime(properties.timestamp) < now() - 7d
| project
    resourceId = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    sub        = subscriptionId,
    rg         = tostring(properties.resourceGroup),
    policy     = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName),
    since      = todatetime(properties.timestamp)
| order by since asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table
Dashboard Limitations and Common Issues
Issue
Cause
Workaround
Dashboard shows 24h-old data
Background scan runs every 24 hours
Run az policy state trigger-scan per subscription to refresh; allow 5–30 min for results
Score is higher than expected after adding exemptions
Exempt resources are removed from the denominator, not counted as Compliant
Normal behavior — the score formula excludes Exempt from both numerator and denominator
New resources show as Not Started for hours
New resources are only evaluated at the next background scan or after trigger-scan
Trigger a scan after deploying resources that should be immediately evaluated
Policy with Effect: Deny shows 100% with 0 resources
Deny blocks creation; no resources exist that could be NonCompliant
Correct — a well-placed Deny policy will always show 100% compliance
Per-resource drill-down missing some assignments
Initiative policies appear under the initiative assignment, not as individual rows
Click the initiative assignment row to see per-policy breakdown within it
Compliance percentage differs between portal and CLI
CLI summarize may use a different scope or filter than the portal view
Use the same scope and filter in CLI as in the portal; check notScopes on assignments
Compliance Dashboard in Defender for Cloud

Microsoft Defender for Cloud (formerly Azure Security Center) extends the Azure Policy compliance dashboard with security-specific views. The Regulatory Compliance blade in Defender for Cloud maps policy states to security controls from frameworks such as CIS Azure Benchmark, NIST SP 800-53, PCI DSS, ISO 27001, and FedRAMP. It shows which specific controls are passing or failing, how many resources back each control, and lets you download a PDF compliance report for auditors. The data source is still PolicyInsights — Defender for Cloud just presents it through a framework control mapping.

# Get security assessments from Defender for Cloud (maps to PolicyInsights)
az security assessment list \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Status:status.code,Resource:resourceDetails.Id}' \
  -o table | head -30

# Get the current regulatory compliance state for a specific standard
az security regulatory-compliance-standards list \
  --query '[].{Standard:name,State:state,Passed:passedControls,Failed:failedControls}' \
  -o table

# Drill into a specific standard to see control-level compliance
az security regulatory-compliance-controls list \
  --standard-name 'Azure-CIS-1.3.0' \
  --query '[].{Control:name,State:state,Passed:passedAssessments,Failed:failedAssessments}' \
  -o table

# Get the assessments (policy results) under a specific control
az security regulatory-compliance-assessments list \
  --standard-name 'Azure-CIS-1.3.0' \
  --control-name '1.1' \
  --query '[].{Assessment:name,State:state,Resource:resourceDetails.Id}' \
  -o table

# Download a compliance report for a standard (returns PDF download URL)
az rest \
  --method post \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Security/regulatoryComplianceStandards/Azure-CIS-1.3.0/generateComplianceReport?api-version=2021-06-01" \
  --query 'reportUrl' -o tsv
Integrating Compliance Data into Azure Monitor Alerts

The compliance dashboard is a pull-based view — it shows what was true at last scan time. For push-based alerting (immediate notification when compliance drops), combine PolicyInsights data with Azure Monitor Log Analytics. The PolicyStateChangeEvents diagnostic stream, when enabled on a subscription, sends compliance state transitions to a Log Analytics workspace where alert rules can fire on new NonCompliant events.

# Enable Policy diagnostic export to Log Analytics
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --name policy-state-to-law \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --workspace $LAW_ID \
  --logs '[
    {"category": "PolicyEvaluationDetails", "enabled": true},
    {"category": "PolicyStateChangeEvents",  "enabled": true}
  ]'

# KQL alert query: fire when NonCompliant count exceeds threshold in last 1h
# (use this as the query in an Azure Monitor Scheduled Query alert rule)
# AzureDiagnostics
# | where ResourceProvider == 'MICROSOFT.POLICYINSIGHTS'
# | where Category == 'PolicyStateChangeEvents'
# | where complianceState_s == 'NonCompliant'
# | where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
# | summarize NewViolations = count() by policyAssignmentName_s, subscriptionId_g
# | where NewViolations > 0

# Create a scheduled query alert rule via CLI
az monitor scheduled-query create \
  --name alert-new-noncompliant \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --scopes $LAW_ID \
  --condition-query "AzureDiagnostics | where Category == 'PolicyStateChangeEvents' and complianceState_s == 'NonCompliant' | summarize count()" \
  --condition-threshold 0 \
  --condition-operator GreaterThan \
  --condition-time-aggregation Count \
  --evaluation-frequency PT5M \
  --window-size PT15M \
  --action-groups $ACTION_GROUP_ID \
  --severity 2
Compliance Reporting with Power BI

For executive-level compliance dashboards, Power BI connected to Azure Resource Graph or Log Analytics provides trend charts, heat maps, and subscription-level roll-ups that the Azure portal dashboard cannot show. The Resource Graph connector for Power BI is the most direct path — it runs KQL queries against the PolicyInsights table and returns a live dataset that refreshes on a schedule.

# Prepare a compliance dataset for Power BI by exporting to a storage account

# Step 1: Export NonCompliant state to a JSON file
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| project
    resourceId       = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    sub              = subscriptionId,
    rg               = tostring(properties.resourceGroup),
    assignment       = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName),
    policy           = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName),
    effect           = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionAction),
    evaluatedAt      = todatetime(properties.timestamp)
" --management-groups mg-corp -o json > compliance_report.json

# Step 2: Upload to a storage account blob
az storage blob upload \
  --account-name $STORAGE_ACCT \
  --container-name compliance-exports \
  --name "report-$(date +%Y%m%d).json" \
  --file compliance_report.json \
  --auth-mode login

# Step 3: In Power BI Desktop, connect via:
# Get Data → Azure → Azure Resource Graph
# Enter the KQL query above directly in the Power BI connector
# Set a daily refresh schedule in Power BI Service

# Alternative: export compliance state CSV for Excel import
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(tostring(properties.complianceState) == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(tostring(properties.complianceState) == 'NonCompliant'),
    Exempt       = countif(tostring(properties.complianceState) == 'Exempt')
    by
    sub        = subscriptionId,
    assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName)
| extend Score = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table > compliance_summary.tsv
Summary

The Azure Policy Compliance Dashboard aggregates PolicyInsights data into a compliance score (Compliant / (Total - Exempt - Not Started)) and supports hierarchical drill-down from management group to resource. The dashboard reflects the last completed background scan — trigger az policy state trigger-scan to get fresher data. For bulk exports and custom reporting, use the PolicyInsights REST API or Resource Graph. Workbooks built on Resource Graph queries provide executive-level trending and cross-subscription aggregations that the built-in dashboard cannot show.
